articles.title: Recent Correspondence Between Schmitt and Noem Indicates OPT Reform Still on DHS Agenda
articles.summary: Senator Eric Schmitt (R-Missouri) recently corresponded with DHS Secretary Kristi Noem regarding reform or termination of the Optional Practical Training (OPT) program. In her response, Secretary Noem referenced a Spring 2025 regulatory agenda item on practical training—posted September 4, 2025—and stated it would proceed through the standard Administrative Procedure Act notice-and-comment process.
articles.insight:
This news signals potential future tightening of U.S. post-study work pathways, which may modestly dampen U.S. appeal for international students prioritizing OPT as a career bridge—though immediate demand shifts are unlikely given the regulatory timeline and lack of finalized policy.  
Admissions teams should track the Spring 2025 DHS regulatory docket for proposed rule language, consult legal advisors on likely implementation timelines, and prepare nuanced messaging that balances U.S. academic strengths with realistic post-graduation planning—without preemptively alarming applicants.

articles.title: US: Student visa issuances fell by -36% in summer 2025; OPT uncertainty among factors affecting international student demand
articles.summary: US student visa issuances fell by 36% in summer 2025, with uncertainty surrounding the Optional Practical Training (OPT) programme cited as a key factor affecting international student demand. The US government has renewed its focus on the OPT programme, and former Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem, who was replaced on 5 March, had previously responded favourably to Senator Eric…
articles.insight:
The 36% drop in US student visa issuances during summer 2025—linked partly to OPT uncertainty—suggests growing caution among international applicants, though it remains unclear whether this reflects temporary policy anxiety or a deeper, sustained shift in destination preferences across key source markets.  
Admissions teams should track real-time application and enrolment trends by region and programme level, while closely monitoring US regulatory developments around OPT, visa processing timelines, and comparative messaging from peer destinations to assess evolving competitive positioning.

articles.title: US visa issuance fell by 36% last summer
articles.summary: New State Department data shows a 36% year-on-year decline in U.S. study visa issuance last summer. Stakeholders attribute this drop to policy volatility under the Trump administration.
articles.insight:
The 36% drop in US study visa issuances last summer—linked to Trump-era policy volatility—may reflect heightened perceived risk among prospective students, though it’s unclear whether this signals sustained demand erosion or short-term administrative disruption amid broader geopolitical and economic factors.  
Admissions teams should track real-time application trends, visa processing timelines, and sentiment shifts in key source markets—noting that competitor nations may gain traction if US visa uncertainty persists, but avoiding premature strategic pivots without corroborating enrollment data.

articles.title: New Zealand expands post-study work opportunities for international students
articles.summary: New Zealand plans to introduce a new Short Term Graduate Work Visa and extend eligibility for the Post Study Work Visa, with implementation scheduled for late 2026. No specific launch date has been confirmed, and the government’s international education strategy emphasizes managed growth over the coming years.
articles.insight:
New Zealand’s planned 2026 visa reforms signal a strategic effort to retain talent post-study, potentially strengthening its appeal—especially among students from Asia and the Pacific—though actual demand shifts depend on implementation details and concurrent policy changes elsewhere.  
Admissions teams should track New Zealand’s final visa criteria, timeline clarity, and comparative post-study work offerings in the UK, Australia, and Canada, while assessing how these developments influence student conversations and application patterns in key source markets over the next 12–18 months.

articles.title: DOS Rulemaking on Subpart C of the Exchange Visitor Regulations
articles.summary: On March 10, 2026, the U.S. Department of State submitted a proposed rule titled “Exchange Visitor Rules: Records” to the Office of Management and Budget for regulatory review. The proposal would replace existing provisions at 22 CFR 62.43 (Extension of program) and 22 CFR 62.45 (Reinstatement to valid program status) to align the Exchange Visitor Regulations with the current functionality of the Student and Exchange Visitor Information System (SEVIS).
articles.insight:
This regulatory update reflects ongoing U.S. efforts to modernize exchange visitor administration via SEVIS, potentially improving processing efficiency for J-1 visa holders—but its direct impact on broader international student demand or F-1 visa pathways remains unclear and likely minimal in the near term.  
Admissions teams should track whether these changes coincide with broader U.S. policy shifts affecting exchange programs, and consider reviewing institutional partnerships with J-1 sponsors to ensure alignment with evolving compliance expectations and student support needs.

articles.title: SEVP Page - Beware of DHS Imposters: Protecting Foreign Students from Scams
articles.summary: A February 19, 2026 SEVP resource titled “Beware of DHS Imposters: Protecting Foreign Students from Scams” warns international students about scammers impersonating U.S. Department of Homeland Security officials to solicit money or personal information. The guidance instructs Designated School Officials to remind students that government agencies do not demand payment by phone, to share scam-prevention information, and to direct affected students to report suspicious contacts and seek assistance from their schools.
articles.insight:
This advisory signals growing risks of fraud targeting international students, potentially amplifying pre-enrolment anxiety in key source markets—though direct impacts on demand or visa policy remain unconfirmed, it may subtly erode trust in U.S. immigration processes among prospective applicants and their families.  
Admissions teams should monitor regional scam-reporting trends and student support queries for early signs of heightened concern, while reviewing orientation materials to reinforce official communication protocols and institutional safeguards against impersonation scams.

articles.title: Webinar for Students Transitioning from F-1 to H-1B
articles.summary: The International Student Resource Center (ISRC) is partnering with Interstride to host a webinar titled “F-1 Student to H-1B Worker: Process and Planning in Turbulent Times” on February 12, 2026, at 1:00 PM ET. The webinar will feature a panel of immigration attorneys and international education professionals who will explain the F-1 to H-1B transition pathway and address recent immigration policy and regulatory changes affecting the process.
articles.insight:
This webinar signals growing institutional concern about post-study work pathway uncertainty, potentially reflecting heightened anxiety among prospective international students—especially from high-application markets—regarding U.S. immigration volatility, though direct demand shifts remain unconfirmed without enrollment or application trend data.  
Admissions teams should track whether similar webinars emerge across peer institutions and monitor source-market social media sentiment around U.S. H-1B lottery outcomes and policy announcements, as these may foreshadow subtle shifts in destination preferences or counseling needs ahead of key application cycles.

articles.title: Dozens of international students who won Chevening scholarships were denied visas
articles.summary: The UK Home Office denied visa applications for dozens of international students who had been awarded Chevening scholarships from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar, and Sudan. This decision followed a prior ban on student visas for nationals from these countries.
articles.insight:
This incident signals potential volatility in UK student visa policy for high-risk origin countries, which may indirectly influence global student demand as applicants reassess destination safety, fairness, and predictability—though direct spillover to other English-speaking nations remains unproven and context-dependent.  
Admissions teams should monitor visa refusal trends, government statements, and agent feedback from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar, and Sudan—noting shifts in application volumes or expressed concerns—while reviewing their own pre-application support and contingency messaging for affected cohorts.

articles.title: Canada and India deepen educational ties; India repositions as an equal player in international education
articles.summary: Canada and India are strengthening their educational ties, reflecting a broader shift in how India is perceived in international education. India’s status as the world’s fastest-growing economy last year, with a 7.5% expansion surpassing that of the US, China, Germany, and Japan, underscores its repositioning as an equal player—not merely an emerging economy—in global education and geopolitics.
articles.insight:
This news signals growing Indian institutional confidence and potential shifts in student mobility patterns, though direct impacts on Western enrolments remain uncertain—India’s economic rise may bolster outbound demand but could also accelerate domestic higher education investment.  
Admissions teams should monitor India-specific scholarship expansions, bilateral credit-transfer agreements, and visa policy adjustments between Canada-India, while assessing whether Canadian partnerships catalyse increased Indian applications to other English-speaking destinations.

articles.title: British Council says student recruitment to UK higher education will get a boost this year from South Asia and the “Trump effect”
articles.summary: The British Council’s latest “5 Trends to Watch” report forecasts that student recruitment to UK higher education will increase this year, driven by growing demand from South Asia and the so-called “Trump effect.” The report notes that demand for UK education will remain resilient despite heightened competition from intra-regional mobility in East Asia and a slowdown in student flows from China.
articles.insight:
This news suggests potential upward pressure on UK student enrolments from South Asia and possible spillover demand from US policy shifts, though the “Trump effect” remains speculative and may reflect sentiment rather than measurable visa or policy changes affecting global student flows.  
Admissions teams should track real-time application data from India, Bangladesh, and Pakistan—and monitor US visa processing times and rhetoric around international education—to assess whether perceived instability is translating into tangible enrolment shifts across destination countries.
